data.title: Overlooking Asian American voters could cost Democrats the midterms
data.description:
It’s no secret that with each election cycle, the costs become higher and the results become tighter. And every election cycle, pundits, party strategists, campaign operatives and consultants scramble to identify the coalition and the hot new silver bullet tactic that will carry Democrats over the finish line, bending over backwards to reinvent the wheel. 

Cycle after cycle, they overlook the fastest-growing community in the nation that is increasingly the margin of victory in key elections: Asian American voters. In battleground states including Arizona, Georgia, Nevada, North Carolina and Pennsylvania, the total number of Asian Americans who cast ballots in 2020 and 2024 exceeded the presidential margin of victory in each state. 

With the 2026 midterms fast approaching, this continued oversight could prove catastrophic for Democrats and a critical opportunity for those willing to invest early and strategically.

In 2020, Asian American voters arguably delivered — or at minimum, helped secure — Biden’s victories in key swing states. Asian American voter turnout increased by nearly 62,000 voters between 2016 and 2020 in Georgia, where Biden carried the state by fewer than 12,000 votes. In Nevada, Asian American votes surged by 66 percent from 2016; in Arizona, by 58 percent, with both states decided by margins of tens of thousands of votes.

However, Asian American support for the Democratic presidential candidate declined from 2020 to 2024, with Trump gaining 10 points among Asian voters in four years. The same communities that delivered Biden’s margins in Georgia, Arizona and Nevada failed to show up in 2024 where sustained grassroots investment was either pulled back, deprioritized or never materialized. 

Let’s also be reminded that the Asian American electorate is not monolithic and treating it as such is a costly mistake campaigns can make. The Asian American electorate encompasses dozens of ethnicities, languages, backgrounds and lived experiences, from multigenerational Filipino families in California, Samoan communities in Nevada, Hmong immigrant enclaves in Minnesota, to second-generation Korean Americans in Georgia. Reaching these voters requires multilingual resources and trusted relationships that cannot be built overnight. 

While Asian American voters are historically a reliable Democratic constituency, they often feel unseen by the Democratic Party and campaigns. In an increasingly polarized political environment where every statewide race comes down to the thinnest of margins, a reliable bloc representing 3 percent to 12 percent of the key state electorate is either your greatest asset or your most preventable loss.

Asian American voters represent 13 percent of the electorate in Nevada, 4.8 percent in Arizona, 4.6 percent in Georgia, 3.8 percent in Pennsylvania and 3.4 percent in Michigan and North Carolina. Nevada and Arizona also saw a surge in Asian American votes, with both states decided by margins of tens of thousands of votes. Notably across all battleground states that year, Asian American turnout increased by 48 percent.

And yet political parties and candidates continue to look away. According to a survey by AAPI Data, 42 percent of Asian American voters said they had not been contacted by either major party or any candidate in 2024. Year after year, cycle after cycle, Asian American voters across the country, especially within communities of battleground states, are feeling like an afterthought when it comes to voter outreach. 

My organization, the Asian American Power Network, works across 12 states, including Nevada, Arizona, Georgia, Pennsylvania, Michigan and North Carolina. We recognize that sustained community organizing is essential to Asian American voters not becoming a side story in key elections. Parachuting in resources around election day is a pattern that has failed other communities before us.

In 2020, Democrats spent twice as much on Spanish-language media in Florida as Republicans, but the majority of that spending came in the final weeks of the campaign. By then, Republicans had already embedded their message. Even ahead of the 2024 elections, top Democrats warned campaign strategists need go beyond “last-minute or superficial” investments of Black voters in Georgia and Michigan, both key battleground states that eventually saw an underperformance by Harris with Black voters.

Asian American voters have spent years proving they are the margin of victory. The numbers are in. The results are documented. Invest in Asian American communities now, or watch the most decisive, reliable, fastest-growing voting bloc in the country stay home.

The question is now whether the Democratic Party will heed the lessons of the past to ensure decisive victory before 2026 makes that answer unavoidable. Invest in Asian American voters. The margin depends on it.

Mary Lou Akai-Ferguson is the executive director of the Asian American Power Network, a national network of state-based organizations.

data.title: Homicide levels trending lowest in more than a century: Research
data.description:
The U.S. is on track to reach a new historic low in the annual homicide rate in 2026, according to a new report released on Thursday. 

The rate of reported homicides in the first half of 2026 was 18 percent lower than it was during the same period in 2025, according to data from 30 cities included in the Council on Criminal Justice’s (CCJ) report on nationwide crime trends. 

This percent change accounted for a total of 215 fewer homicides this year. 

The CCJ report at the end of last year estimated that the U.S. homicide rate in 2025 dropped to around 4 per 1,000 people. This would be the lowest rate since 1900, if confirmed by FBI data. The independent research center’s new report on crime statistics for the first half of this year indicates an improvement on this trend. 

Reported homicides are now 51 percent lower than they were from an all-time high in the first half of 2022. Carjacking, residential burglary and motor vehicle theft reports have also all decreased substantially during this time period, according to the CCJ report.

Additionally, the 2026 homicide rate is 31 percent lower across these 30 cities than it was in the first half of 2019. Twenty-six of the sample cities have reported decreased homicide rates since 2019. Lincoln, Neb., experienced a 100 percent reduction in reported homicides during this time period. 

However, four cities reported rates for the first half of 2026 that were higher than their 2019 reports. Norfolk, Va., increased by 36 percent, Pittsburgh, Pa., by 4 percent and Minneapolis, Minn. by 26 percent. Austin, Texas, reported the largest spike from 2019 to 2026 with a 74 percent hike in reported homicides. 

The CCJ researchers noted that “there is no single or easy explanation for the broad drop in crime” displayed in this report, pointing to a mixture of changes in demographics, behavior and public policy across the nation. 

“As we continue to gain deeper understanding of the dynamics behind the nation’s crime trends, communities should invest in strategies that are supported by rigorous research and producing tangible results on the ground,” the researchers wrote in the report. 

“For families who have lost loved ones to violence, even the continuing progress of 2026 is of little comfort,” they concluded. “Keeping that in mind will help the United States stay on track toward a future of even greater public safety.”

data.title: Watch live: House Democrats outline agenda as midterm elections loom
data.description:
House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) and Democratic leaders will speak with reporters Thursday morning to outline the party’s accomplishments and goals as the midterms loom.

Since this congressional term began, lawmakers on the left have focused much of their efforts on combatting President Trump’s crackdown on immigration, economic agenda as affordability concerns rise and the ongoing conflict with Iran. The House earlier Thursday passed a Democratic-backed war powers resolution aimed at limiting the president’s military action against Tehran.

The press conference is scheduled to begin at 11 a.m. EDT.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Ocasio-Cortez leading potential 2028 Democratic presidential nominees in New Hampshire survey
data.description:
Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.) is leading a field of possible candidates for the 2028 Democratic presidential nomination in New Hampshire, according to a new poll.

In the Granite State Poll from the University of New Hampshire, Ocasio-Cortez garnered 22 percent backing from respondents, with former Transportation Secretary Pete Buttigieg behind her at 21 percent and Sen. Mark Kelly (D-Ariz.) trailing at 9 percent.

Last October, Buttigieg led the field with 19 percent support, followed by California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) at 15 percent and Ocasio-Cortez at 14 percent. A few months later in February, Buttigieg maintained his lead with 20 percent and Newsom and Ocasio-Cortez tied at 15 percent.

The rise of Ocasio-Cortez, a democratic socialist, in New Hampshire comes alongside progressives stepping into the national political spotlight in the last year with insurgent campaigns against more establishment figures. One of those campaigns was run by New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani (D), who has now become one of the most prominent and widely discussed members of the Democratic Party.

There have also been calls for Ocasio-Cortez to challenge Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) for his seat two years from now, following democratic socialist candidates winning victories in Democratic primaries in late June.

In December, Ocasio-Cortez highlighted a poll showing her beating Vice President Vance in a hypothetical presidential match-up.

The Granite State Poll, from the University of New Hampshire Survey Center, took place from July 15 and 20, featuring 648 likely 2028 Democratic presidential primary voters and a margin of sampling error of plus or minus 3.8 percentage points.

data.title: Stephen A. Smith: Nolan Wells death 'resonates' with Black community
data.description:
Stephen A. Smith said Wednesday the death of 18-year-old Nolan Wells “resonates particularly in the Black community.”

The political and sports commentator said to NewsNation’s Chris Cuomo Wednesday that the circumstances and lack of answers surrounding the case are “highly suspicious.”

The conversation started when Cuomo asked why this case has struck a chord, involving prominent civil rights figures like attorney Benjamin Crump and Rev. Al Sharpton.

“There was a Black dude who was the only Black dude in attendance and he’s the only person, the human being that didn’t come home alive,” Smith said.

Wells’s death is currently under investigation after he was found dead following a July 4 boating trip with his friends off the coast of Mississippi. His family reported him missing on Independence Day around 11 p.m. after a call from Wells’s friend.

Photos have appeared to show Wells as the only Black person in attendance; however, it is still unconfirmed. 

The family of the teen commissioned an independent autopsy in Washington, D.C., where Crump announced during the NAACP Convention on Wednesday that preliminary results said the death was “undetermined” and pending a further investigation.

“The bottom line is, is that if you sent your son out and they go on a boat trip with these guys, and he’s the only one that doesn’t come back alive, and he’s the only Black individual that was there, you better believe that the Black community is going to be up in arms,” he added.

data.title: Oil prices spike as Houthis say they struck Red Sea tankers
data.description:
Oil prices have jumped as the conflict with Iran escalates, this time with attacks on ships in the Red Sea by the Houthi militia group alligned with Tehran.

On Thursday, international benchmark Brent crude crossed the $100 mark, with futures trading at about $101 per barrel, up more than $25 from where it was trading during the U.S.-Iran ceasefire in July.

U.S. benchmark WTI crude futures were trading at about $92 per barrel, also up about $25 from their early July lows.

The war has sent oil prices fluctuating as the violence has threatened shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, a channel between Iran and the Arabian peninsula through which much of the world’s oil typically flows.

Meanwhile, Houthi rebels in Yemen said Thursday that they attacked two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea, which is situated on the other side of the Arabian peninsula. 

Attacks here threaten the oil being shipped through this route after Iran blocked off the Strait of Hormuz.

The Houthis, which are backed by Iran, said they used ballistic and cruise missiles to target ships after their blockade of a key Red Sea chokepoint, the Bab el-Mandeb Strait.

The price of gasoline in the United States was also up on Thursday, averaging about $4.09  per gallon nationally, according to AAA.

data.title: Four Republicans help House Democrats pass Iran war powers resolution
data.description:
The House on Thursday passed a Democratic-backed war powers resolution aimed at limiting President Trump’s military action in Iran.

The lower chamber voted 214-208 to pass the resolution, sponsored by Rep. Pramila Jayapal (D-Wash.). Four Republicans joined all Democrats in supporting the measure.

The latest push comes amid a rising death toll among U.S. service members during renewed fighting between the U.S. and Tehran. Eighteen troops have died since the U.S. and Israel launched joint strikes against Iran in February. 

“This is a vote of conscience. This is a vote that requires us to find the strength to do what is right for the American people, and to send the clearest, even stronger message to this president of the United States that the United States Congress, the House of Representatives, is reasserting our authority over war. This war must end,” Jayapal said in remarks on the House floor Wednesday ahead of the vote. 

Both chambers of Congress approved similar resolutions last month, indicating growing frustrations from lawmakers over the Trump administration’s handling of this conflict. 

Reps. Tom Barrett (R-Mich.), Warren Davidson (R-Ohio), Thomas Massie (Ky.) and Brian Fitzpatrick (Pa.) joined all Democrats in voting for a war powers resolution in June. GOP Sens. Rand Paul (Ky.), Susan Collins (Maine), Lisa Murkowski (Alaska) and Bill Cassidy (La.) backed a war powers resolution that same month over in the Senate.

Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) announced in remarks Wednesday that Senate Democrats will force a war powers resolution on the floor Thursday.

“Every senator will have to make a choice,” Schumer said. “You cannot claim to support the troops while leaving them trapped in war with no mission and no end. You cannot call it a victory while the battlefield keeps expanding, and you cannot hide the wounded and call it peace.”

On Wednesday, Trump participated in a dignified transfer of the service members killed in Jordan and Iraq amid the U.S. war with Iran. 

The president warned Tehran against attacks against commercial ships in the Strait of Hormuz, a major oil shipping corridor, in a Truth Social post on Wednesday. The U.S. has cited these attacks as a breach of the ceasefire agreement that each side agreed to last month. 

“From this point forward, any time the Islamic Republic of Iran shoots at a ship in the Strait of Hormuz, whether it be by Missile, Rocket, Drone, or any other device or weapon, the United States will bomb and destroy ONE BRIDGE OR POWER PLANT, including those located next to, or in, the Capital City of Tehran,” Trump wrote.

data.title: Attorney general nomination receives intense scrutiny
data.description:
The role of the country’s top lawyer came into sharp focus last week as the Senate Judiciary Committee conducted two days of hearings on the nomination of Todd Blanche as U.S. attorney general.  

Committee members of both parties peppered Blanche and other witnesses with questions about his past conduct both as President Donald Trump’s personal defense attorney, his previous role as a U.S. attorney in the Department of Justice, and, most recently, as deputy attorney general and now acting attorney general following the resignation of Pam Bondi earlier this year.

Not surprisingly, the two most outspoken Republican skeptics on the committee were Sen. John Cornyn (R-Texas), who lost his reelection primary to a Trump-backed challenger, and Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) who is retiring. Both seemed somewhat placated by the end of the second hearing. With the death of Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.), the committee ratio stood at 11 to 10 last week, meaning the negative vote of a single Republican could doom the nomination until the Graham committee slot is filled.

At the center of the committee’s concern was the belief that Blanche was more loyal to the president and his whims than he was to the Constitution. He assured the committee he would always put America’s interests above those of his former client, saying his service as Trump’s personal lawyer for less than two years “does not define my career, nor does it define me.” He added, “I am his lawyer,” then quickly corrected himself: “I was his lawyer.”

That slip, though, was actually more accurate than Freudian, since the Judiciary Act of 1789 that created the office of attorney general designates the attorney general as the president’s chief legal adviser. Specifically, the statute empowers the attorney general as the chief law enforcement officer of the federal government, charged with prosecuting and conducting “all suits in the Supreme Court,” and to “give his advice and opinion upon questions of law when required by the president” or “when requested by the heads of any of the departments.”    

The Senate confirmation process for principal executive officials, ambassadors and federal judges is derived from Article II, section 2, clause 2, of the Constitution — the “appointments clause.” It gives to the president, “by and with the advice and consent of the Senate,” the power to “appoint ambassadors, other public ministers and consuls, judges of the Supreme Court, and all other officers of the United States, whose appointments are not otherwise provided for, and which shall be established by law.”

The number of positions subject to Senate confirmation has grown exponentially since 1789. According to the Office of Personnel Management, by 2020 it had grown to 1,118 positions in agencies and departments subject to the Senate’s advice and consent. The bulk of those are in the Departments of State (254) and Justice (218).

The Department of Justice has been a lightning rod of controversy over the past century, primarily because it is at the heart of dealing with cases and controversies. Some of those controversies have put the department and its head in a less than favorable light. From March 1919 to March 2021, President Woodrow Wilson’s attorney general, A. Mitchell Palmer, initiated what came to be known as the “Palmer Raids,” or “Red Scare,” in which thousands of people suspected of being socialists or communists were rounded up and imprisoned for months. 

Palmer’s successor as attorney general, Harry M. Daugherty, was removed from office by President Warren Harding’s successor, President Calvin Coolidge, after being implicated in the Teapot Dome scandal, involving the illegal transfer of oil leases in Wyoming.          

Four decades later, in the 1970s, John Mitchell, who was President Richard M. Nixon’s attorney general, became involved in the Watergate scandal, stemming from a break-in at the Democratic National Committee headquarters, illegal wiretaps and massive coverup attempt. Mitchell was found guilty of conspiracy, obstruction of justice and perjury, spent 19 months in prison, and lost his law license.

Other scandals involving attorneys general continued into the 21st century. Based on a History News Network account, of the 20 attorneys general appointed since John Mitchell, five have been tainted by scandals. One can understand why the Senate is being careful in vetting the current nomination of Todd Blanche for that office.

Alexander Hamilton observed in Federalist 76 that requiring Senate concurrence in presidential appointments, “would be an excellent check upon a spirit of favoritism in the president and would tend greatly to prevent the appointment of unfit characters.” The Constitution’s parchment barriers are indeed fragile.

Don Wolfensberger is a 28-year congressional staff veteran culminating as chief of staff of the House Rules Committee in 1995. He is author of “Congress and the People: Deliberative Democracy on Trial” (2000), and “Changing Cultures in Congress: From Fair Play to Power Plays” (2018).

data.title: Appeals court blocks Trump administration from revoking TPS for Haitians until at least Monday
data.description:
A federal appeals court has temporarily stopped the Trump administration from ending deportation protections for hundreds of thousands of Haitian migrants living in the U.S., at least for another few days.

More than 300,000 Haitians were set to lose their Temporary Protected Status (TPS) on Friday following a Supreme Court ruling that found the Trump administration could terminate the designations for individuals from the island and Syria. Work permits were also set to expire then.

But a three-judge panel for the U.S. Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit on Wednesday denied the administration’s request to stay a lower court order that postponed the TPS revocations while legal challenges continued.

The judges, two of whom were nominated by former President Biden and one by President Trump in his first term, blocked the federal government from stripping TPS before at least July 27.

“They have not shown that a stay of the district court’s February 2, 2026 order pending the Supreme Court’s return of the case to this court is warranted, particularly given that the case will be returned to this court on July 27, 2026,” the brief order stated.

The Trump administration asked the court in early July to halt the lower court order, arguing it was erroneous and there was “no reason to prolong the effect” of the interim decision given the Supreme Court’s reversal.  

“Each day the district court’s unlawful decision remains in effect irreversibly blocks the Executive Branch by preventing it from implementing its lawful decision,” the Justice Department wrote in its filing.

The Department of Homeland Security did not immediately respond to The Hill’s request for comment.

The Supreme Court ruled in a 6-3 decision, along ideological lines, that federal judges had no authority to weigh in on the challengers’ non-constitutional claims, effectively allowing the Trump administration to proceed with its controversial immigration policy.

TPS was created in 1990 to provide temporary, lawful immigration status and protection from deportation to nationals of certain foreign countries who are already in the U.S. when conditions in their home countries make it unsafe for them to return.

The Trump administration has argued that conditions in Haiti no longer justify the designation, despite a State Department advisory warning Americans against traveling to the Caribbean nation due to the risk of “crime, kidnapping, terrorism, unrest, and limited health care.”

The administration is also pushing to rescind TPS for more than one million holders from nearly a dozen other countries, whose fate now remains uncertain in the wake of the high court’s decision.

data.title: Live updates: Houthis hit Red Sea tankers; Congress votes on separate war powers resolutions
data.description:
President Trump is threatening to intensify the war with Iran as some Republican lawmakers urge him to “finish the job,” but there is also widespread skepticism on Capitol Hill on both sides of the aisle about whether he can deliver a victory.

The House convenes Thursday morning to vote on a Democrat-sponsored war powers resolution. The Senate will vote on a separate but similar resolution. They come as Trump’s support for the Iran conflict is reaching a new low among his base.

The Iran-backed Houthi rebels in Yemen on Thursday attacked two oil tankers in the Red Sea, a ramping up of the conflict since the group announced a blockade of Saudi ships attempting to go through the Bab el-Mandeb Strait earlier this week. 

The Senate Judiciary Committee is scheduled to discuss the nomination of acting Attorney General Todd Blanche to take the role permanently. Republican Sens. John Cornyn (Texas) and Thom Tillis (N.C.) have not committed to backing him, and their opposition could sink the nomination. A vote is not expected at this point.

And Dr. Erica Schwartz faces a vote in the Senate Health, Education, Labor and Pensions (HELP) Committee for her nomination to be director of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

The Energy Department announced Wednesday a nuclear cooperation deal with Saudi Arabia, and it drew swift backlash. Insiders can read more here.

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt will hold a press briefing today at 11 a.m. EDT. Following, Trump is set to visit the Environmental Protection Agency and will make a statement about the Ratepayer Protection Pledge, a pledge he issued in March in which U.S. artificial intelligence (AI) companies cover the electricity costs for their data centers, not consumers.

Follow live below.
